title: Verifying Trigonometric Identities
objectives: Distinguish between conditional equations and identities
Develop fluency in transforming trigonometric expressions

Master techniques for verifying identities: converting to sine/cosine, 
 using conjugates, factoring, and combining fractions

content.title: Truth That Endures
content.content:
The equation $2x + 3 = 7$ is true only when $x = 2$. But the equation 
$\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ is true for *every* value of $\theta$. 
This distinction between conditional truth and universal truth mirrors a 
spiritual reality. Many things in life depend on circumstances—our moods, 
our circumstances, our earthly fortunes. But the character of God, as revealed 
in Scripture, remains constant: "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today 
and forever" (Hebrews 13:8). As you learn to work with identities—statements 
that hold universally—consider what truths in your own life remain unchanged 
regardless of circumstance.

content.text:
When you first learned algebra, you solved equations by finding specific values 
that made statements true. The equation $x^2 - 4 = 0$ has solutions $x = \pm 2$, 
and for any other value, the equation fails. A *trigonometric identity* 
is fundamentally different: it's an equation involving trigonometric functions 
that is true for *all* values where both sides are defined.

Consider the equation $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$. This isn't 
a puzzle to solve—there's no special angle that makes it work. Rather, it's 
a statement about the *structure* of the tangent function. Tangent is 
defined as the ratio of sine to cosine, so this equation holds for every angle 
where cosine isn't zero.

content.title: Trigonometric Identity
content.content:
A **trigonometric identity** is an equation involving trigonometric 
functions that is true for all values of the variable(s) for which both 
sides of the equation are defined. The set of values where an expression 
is defined is called its **domain**.

content.text:
The key phrase is "where both sides are defined." For instance, 
$\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$ is an identity, but we 
must exclude values where $\cos\theta = 0$ (namely, odd multiples of 
$\frac{\pi}{2}$). At those points, neither side of the equation exists.

Before we can verify complex identities, we need a toolkit—a collection 
of basic identities that serve as building blocks. You've encountered most 
of these before, but let's gather them systematically.

content.title: Fundamental Trigonometric Identities
content.content:
**Reciprocal Identities:**

$$
\csc\theta = \frac{1}{\sin\theta}, \quad 
\sec\theta = \frac{1}{\cos\theta}, \quad 
\cot\theta = \frac{1}{\tan\theta}
$$

**Quotient Identities:**

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}, \quad 
\cot\theta = \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}
$$

**Pythagorean Identities:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta &= 1 \\
1 + \tan^2\theta &= \sec^2\theta \\
1 + \cot^2\theta &= \csc^2\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Cofunction Identities:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta\right) &= \cos\theta & 
\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta\right) &= \sin\theta \\
\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta\right) &= \cot\theta &
\cot\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta\right) &= \tan\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Even-Odd Identities:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin(-\theta) &= -\sin\theta & \text{(sine is odd)} \\
\cos(-\theta) &= \cos\theta & \text{(cosine is even)} \\
\tan(-\theta) &= -\tan\theta & \text{(tangent is odd)}
\end{aligned}$$

content.caption:
The Pythagorean identity visualized on the unit circle. For any 
angle $\theta$, the point $P$ lies on the unit circle, so the horizontal 
and vertical legs of the right triangle satisfy $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$.
content.alt:
The Pythagorean identity visualized on the unit circle. For any 
angle $\theta$, the point $P$ lies on the unit circle, so the horizontal 
and vertical legs of the right triangle satisfy $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$.

content.caption:
The fundamental trigonometric identities and their relationships. 
The Pythagorean identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ generates the 
other two Pythagorean forms by division. The reciprocal and quotient 
identities define relationships between the six functions.
content.alt:
The fundamental trigonometric identities and their relationships. 
The Pythagorean identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ generates the 
other two Pythagorean forms by division. The reciprocal and quotient 
identities define relationships between the six functions.

content.text:
Figure [fig:pythagorean-identity] shows why the fundamental Pythagorean 
identity is true: on the unit circle, every point has coordinates 
$(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$, and since the radius is 1, the Pythagorean theorem 
gives us $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ directly.

The Pythagorean identities deserve special attention. The first one, 
$\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$, comes directly from the Pythagorean 
theorem applied to the unit circle. If a point $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ 
lies on the unit circle, then $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1^2$.

The other two Pythagorean identities follow from dividing the first by 
$\cos^2\theta$ or $\sin^2\theta$:
$$
\frac{\sin^2\theta}{\cos^2\theta} + \frac{\cos^2\theta}{\cos^2\theta} = 
\frac{1}{\cos^2\theta} \implies \tan^2\theta + 1 = \sec^2\theta
$$

Verifying an identity is not quite like solving an equation. When you 
solve $2x + 3 = 7$, you perform the same operations on both sides to 
isolate $x$. When you verify an identity, you typically work on 
*one side only*, transforming it until it matches the other side.

content.title: Never ``cross-multiply'' in identity proofs
content.content:
A common error is treating an identity verification like an equation 
and performing operations on both sides simultaneously. This approach 
is logically flawed because you're *assuming* the identity is true 
to prove it's true—circular reasoning. Instead, start with one side 
and use valid algebraic and trigonometric manipulations to transform 
it into the other side.

content.text:
Think of it this way: if someone claims that two different routes 
lead to the same destination, you don't need to walk both routes 
simultaneously. You can start at one end and show it leads to the 
same place as the other.

content.title: Verifying Trigonometric Identities
content.content:
1. **Start with the more complicated side.** It's generally 
 easier to simplify a complex expression than to make a simple one 
 more complex.

2. **Convert everything to sines and cosines.** This 
 provides a common language and often reveals hidden simplifications.

3. **Look for Pythagorean identity opportunities.** 
 Expressions like $1 - \sin^2\theta$ or $\sec^2\theta - 1$ are 
 immediate candidates.

4. **Factor when possible.** Difference of squares, 
 common factors, and trinomial factoring all remain useful.

5. **Multiply by conjugates.** When you see $1 + \sin\theta$, 
 $1 - \cos\theta$, or similar expressions in denominators, multiplying 
 by the conjugate often helps.

6. **Combine fractions.** Finding common denominators can 
 transform complex expressions into simpler ones.

7. **Work toward the target.** Keep the goal in mind and 
 look for opportunities to move closer to it.

content.title: Pattern Recognition Quick Reference
content.content:
When simplifying trigonometric expressions, recognize these common patterns:

{1.5}

| When you see… | Try this identity |
| $1 - \sin^2\theta$ or $1 - \cos^2\theta$ | Pythagorean: $= \cos^2\theta$ or $= \sin^2\theta$ |
| $\sec^2\theta - 1$ or $1 + \tan^2\theta$ | Pythagorean: $= \tan^2\theta$ or $= \sec^2\theta$ |
| $\csc^2\theta - 1$ or $1 + \cot^2\theta$ | Pythagorean: $= \cot^2\theta$ or $= \csc^2\theta$ |
| $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta$ | Pythagorean: $= 1$ |
| $\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$ or $\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}$ | Quotient: $= \tan\theta$ or $= \cot\theta$ |
| $\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}$ or $\dfrac{1}{\cos\theta}$ | Reciprocal: $= \csc\theta$ or $= \sec\theta$ |
| $(1 - \sin\theta)(1 + \sin\theta)$ | Difference of squares: $= \cos^2\theta$ |
| $(1 - \cos\theta)(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Difference of squares: $= \sin^2\theta$ |
| $(\sec\theta - \tan\theta)(\sec\theta + \tan\theta)$ | Difference of squares: $= 1$ |
| $\sin(-\theta)$ or $\cos(-\theta)$ | Even-odd: $= -\sin\theta$ or $= \cos\theta$ |
| $\tan(-\theta)$ | Even-odd: $= -\tan\theta$ |

**Memory tip:** The Pythagorean identities are the workhorses of simplification. 
When stuck, ask: "Can I create a $1 - \sin^2\theta$ or $1 - \cos^2\theta$?"

content.caption:
A strategic flowchart for verifying identities. Start with the 
more complex side and choose your approach based on the structure of 
the expression.
content.alt:
A strategic flowchart for verifying identities. Start with the 
more complex side and choose your approach based on the structure of 
the expression.

content.text: Let's work through several identities, demonstrating various techniques.

content.title: Converting to sines and cosines
content.solution:
We'll work with the left side, converting tangent to its definition:
$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta\cos\theta &= \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} \cdot \cos\theta \\
&= \sin\theta \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$
The $\cos\theta$ in the numerator and denominator cancel (for values 
where $\cos\theta \neq 0$), leaving us with $\sin\theta$.

content.title: Using a Pythagorean identity
content.solution:
The numerator $1 - \sin^2\theta$ immediately suggests the Pythagorean 
identity. Since $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$, we have 
$1 - \sin^2\theta = \cos^2\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1 - \sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta} &= \frac{\cos^2\theta}{\cos\theta} \\
&= \cos\theta \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Multiplying by a conjugate
content.solution:
The left side has $1 + \cos\theta$ in the denominator. To transform 
it, we multiply by the conjugate $\dfrac{1 - \cos\theta}{1 - \cos\theta}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sin\theta}{1 + \cos\theta} &= 
\frac{\sin\theta}{1 + \cos\theta} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{1 - \cos\theta} \\
&= \frac{\sin\theta(1 - \cos\theta)}{1 - \cos^2\theta}
\end{aligned}$$
Now we recognize the denominator: $1 - \cos^2\theta = \sin^2\theta$.
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{\sin\theta(1 - \cos\theta)}{\sin^2\theta} \\
&= \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Combining fractions
Factoring difference of squares
content.solution:
The left side is a difference of fourth powers, which factors as a 
difference of squares:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sec^4\theta - \tan^4\theta 
&= (\sec^2\theta)^2 - (\tan^2\theta)^2 \\
&= (\sec^2\theta + \tan^2\theta)(\sec^2\theta - \tan^2\theta)
\end{aligned}$$
Now, from the Pythagorean identity $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$, 
we get $\sec^2\theta - \tan^2\theta = 1$. Therefore:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= (\sec^2\theta + \tan^2\theta)(1) \\
&= \sec^2\theta + \tan^2\theta \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: A multi-step verification
content.solution:
This problem requires converting to sines and cosines and then 
simplifying systematically. Let's start with the numerator:
$$
\tan\theta + \cot\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} + 
\frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \frac{\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta}{\sin\theta\cos\theta}
= \frac{1}{\sin\theta\cos\theta}
$$

Now the denominator:
$$
\sec\theta\csc\theta = \frac{1}{\cos\theta} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin\theta} 
= \frac{1}{\sin\theta\cos\theta}
$$

Putting it together:
$$
\frac{\tan\theta + \cot\theta}{\sec\theta\csc\theta} = 
\frac{\frac{1}{\sin\theta\cos\theta}}{\frac{1}{\sin\theta\cos\theta}} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

content.title: Working with secant and cosecant
content.solution:
Let's work with the left side and convert to sines and cosines:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sec\theta - \cos\theta &= \frac{1}{\cos\theta} - \cos\theta \\
&= \frac{1 - \cos^2\theta}{\cos\theta} \\
&= \frac{\sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta}
\end{aligned}$$
Now we rewrite this to match the target:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} \cdot \sin\theta \\
&= \tan\theta\sin\theta \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Creating a common structure
content.solution:
This is parallel to the previous example. On the left:
$$\begin{aligned}
\csc\theta - \sin\theta &= \frac{1}{\sin\theta} - \sin\theta \\
&= \frac{1 - \sin^2\theta}{\sin\theta} \\
&= \frac{\cos^2\theta}{\sin\theta} \\
&= \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} \cdot \cos\theta \\
&= \cot\theta\cos\theta \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Patterns in God's Creation
content.content:
Notice the beautiful symmetry between Examples [ex:sec-csc] and 
[ex:common-structure]. By interchanging sine and cosine (and 
correspondingly, tangent and cotangent, secant and cosecant), we get 
parallel truths. This duality—the cofunctions—reflects a deeper 
order in mathematics. The Psalmist wrote, "The heavens declare the 
glory of God; the skies proclaim the work of his hands" (Psalm 19:1). 
Perhaps the elegant patterns of trigonometry also whisper of a 
Designer who delights in order and beauty.

content.text: As you practice verifying identities, watch out for these frequent errors:

content.title: Algebraic Errors
content.content:
- $\sin^2\theta \neq \sin\theta^2$ (the first means 
 $(\sin\theta)^2$; the second is ambiguous)

- $\sin(\theta + \phi) \neq \sin\theta + \sin\phi$ (this is 
 the "freshman's dream" error—we'll learn the correct formula in Section [sec:sum-difference])

- $\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta + \cos\theta} \neq \csc\theta + \sec\theta$ 
 (you cannot distribute a reciprocal over addition)

content.problem:
True or False? Justify your answer.

1. An identity is an equation true for all values in the domain.

2. To verify an identity, you should work on both sides simultaneously.

3. $\sin^2\theta = 1 - \cos^2\theta$ is an identity.

4. $\sin\theta = \cos\theta$ is an identity.

5. $\tan^2\theta + 1 = \sec^2\theta$ is an identity.

6. $\sin\theta + \cos\theta = 1$ is an identity.
Explain the difference between an identity and a conditional equation. Give an example of each.
Why can't you verify an identity by substituting a single specific value (like $\theta = 30°$)?
Explain why starting with the more complicated side is usually the better strategy when verifying identities.

Why is it logically incorrect to "cross-multiply" when verifying an identity?


Simplifying Expressions

Simplify to a single trigonometric function or constant.

1. $\sin\theta\csc\theta$

2. $\cos\theta\sec\theta$

3. $\tan\theta\cot\theta$

4. $\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\csc\theta}$

5. $\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sec\theta}$

6. $\dfrac{\tan\theta}{\cot\theta}$

Simplify to a single trigonometric function.

1. $\tan\theta\cos\theta$

2. $\cot\theta\sin\theta$

3. $\sin\theta\sec\theta$

4. $\cos\theta\csc\theta$

5. $\dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta} + \cos\theta$

6. $\dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{\sin\theta} + \sin\theta$

Simplify to a single trigonometric function.

1. $\dfrac{1 - \cos^2\theta}{\sin\theta}$

2. $\dfrac{1 - \sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta}$

3. $\dfrac{\tan\theta}{\sec\theta}$

4. $\dfrac{\cot\theta}{\csc\theta}$

5. $\sec\theta - \cos\theta$ (simplify fully)

6. $\csc\theta - \sin\theta$ (simplify fully)

Verify each identity using the Pythagorean identities.

1. $\dfrac{1 - \sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta} = \cos\theta$

2. $\dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{1 - \cos\theta} = 1 + \cos\theta$

3. $\dfrac{1 - \cos^2\theta}{1 - \sin\theta} = \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{1 - \sin\theta}$

4. $\dfrac{\tan^2\theta}{\sec\theta + 1} = \sec\theta - 1$

Verify each identity by combining fractions.

1. $\dfrac{1}{1 - \sin\theta} + \dfrac{1}{1 + \sin\theta} = 2\sec^2\theta$

2. $\dfrac{1}{1 - \cos\theta} + \dfrac{1}{1 + \cos\theta} = 2\csc^2\theta$

3. $\dfrac{\sin\theta}{1 - \cos\theta} + \dfrac{1 - \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = 2\csc\theta$

4. $\dfrac{\cos\theta}{1 + \sin\theta} + \dfrac{1 + \sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = 2\sec\theta$

On an inclined plane at angle $\theta$, the component of gravitational force 
parallel to the plane is $F_\parallel = mg\sin\theta$ and perpendicular is 
$F_\perp = mg\cos\theta$. Show that $F_\parallel^2 + F_\perp^2 = (mg)^2$ 
using the Pythagorean identity.
content.label: Physics: Inclined Plane

content.problem:
In AC circuits, the power factor is $\cos\phi$ where $\phi$ is the phase angle. 
Show that $\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi = 1$ implies that if the power factor is $0.8$, 
then $\sin\phi = 0.6$ (assuming $0 \le \phi \le \frac{\pi}{2}$).
content.label: Electrical Engineering

content.problem:
A surveyor uses the identity $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$ to 
calculate angles. If $\sin\theta = 0.6$ and $\cos\theta = 0.8$, verify that 
$\tan\theta = 0.75$ using the quotient identity.
content.label: Surveying

content.problem:
In rotation transformations, the identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ 
ensures that rotation matrices preserve distances. Verify this identity 
geometrically: if a point $(r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$ lies on a circle of 
radius $r$, show that its distance from the origin is indeed $r$.
content.label: Computer Graphics

content.problem:
A ship travels at speed $v$ on a bearing that makes angle $\theta$ with the 
east-west axis. Its eastward velocity is $v\cos\theta$ and northward velocity 
is $v\sin\theta$. Show that the total speed satisfies 
$\sqrt{(v\cos\theta)^2 + (v\sin\theta)^2} = v$.
content.label: Navigation

content.problem:
Use a graphing utility to graph both sides of the equation 
$\dfrac{\sin x}{1 + \cos x} = \dfrac{1 - \cos x}{\sin x}$ on the same screen. 
What do you observe? Does this prove the identity? Explain.

Graph $y = \sin^2 x + \cos^2 x$ for $-2\pi \le x \le 2\pi$. 
What do you observe? Explain why this confirms the Pythagorean identity.

Use a calculator to verify that $\tan^2 37° + 1 = \sec^2 37°$. 
Round to four decimal places.

Graph $y_1 = \tan x \cos x$ and $y_2 = \sin x$ on the same screen. 
Do the graphs appear identical? Where might they differ?


Error Analysis

Describe and correct the error in this "proof":

Verifying $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$:
quote
"$\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$

Cross-multiply: $\tan\theta \cdot \cos\theta = \sin\theta$

This is true, so the identity is verified." (What's wrong?)
quote

Describe and correct the error:

Finding $\cos\theta$ when $\sin\theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$:
quote
"$\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$

$\cos^2\theta = 1 - \dfrac{9}{25} = \dfrac{16}{25}$

$\cos\theta = \dfrac{4}{5}$" (What's incomplete?)
quote

Describe and correct the error:

Simplifying $\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta + \cos\theta}$:
quote
"$\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta + \cos\theta} = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta} + \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta} = \csc\theta + \sec\theta$" 
quote

Describe and correct the error:

Simplifying $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta + \tan^2\theta$:
quote
"$= 1 + \tan^2\theta = 1 + 1 = 2$" 
quote


Challenge Problems

Verify: $\dfrac{\sin\theta + \tan\theta}{\cot\theta + \csc\theta} = \sin\theta\tan\theta$

 Prove: $(\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2 + (\sin\theta - \cos\theta)^2 = 2$

 Prove: $\sec^2\theta + \csc^2\theta = \sec^2\theta \csc^2\theta$

 Prove that $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$ by starting with 
$\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ and dividing both sides by $\cos^2\theta$. 
State the domain restriction.

 Consider the identity $\dfrac{1 - \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{1 + \cos\theta}$.

1. Verify this identity algebraically.

2. For what values of $\theta$ is this identity undefined?

3. Explain why multiplying by the conjugate works in this proof.
